#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Build a 4-revision substep ladder for manual squash inspection.
#
# State after this script:
#   bookmark "base" -> empty commit (no counter.txt)
#   r1   counter.txt = "line 1 count 0"
#   r2   counter.txt = "line 1 count 1"
#   r3   counter.txt = "line 1 count 2"
#   r4   counter.txt = "line 1 count 3"   (@ sits here)
#
# Then run squash recipes by hand from /tmp/substep-test, e.g.:
#
#   jj squash --from "base..@-" --into @                            # downward
#
#   jj squash --from "base..@" --into base --ignore-immutable       # upward
#   jj edit @-

set -euo pipefail

ROOT=/tmp/substep-test

rm -rf "$ROOT"
mkdir -p "$ROOT"
cd "$ROOT"

jj git init --colocate >/dev/null

#jj describe -m "base (empty)" >/dev/null
jj bookmark create base -r @
#jj new >/dev/null

echo "count 0" > counter.txt
jj describe -m "count 0" >/dev/null

jj new >/dev/null
echo "count 1" > counter.txt
jj describe -m "count 1" >/dev/null

jj new >/dev/null
echo "count 2" > counter.txt
jj describe -m "count 2" >/dev/null

jj new >/dev/null
echo "count 3" > counter.txt
jj describe -m "count 3" >/dev/null

echo
echo "::: cwd=$ROOT"
echo
jj log -r "all()"
echo "----- counter.txt -----"
cat counter.txt

content.text:
Reviewed all the template files plus verified the broken-link suspicions. Verdict: the **de-duplication is mostly well done** (the `cargo --locked` content correctly consolidated into README, layered AGENTS.md→cycle-protocol.md pointers), but the reorg **left a batch of dangling links and one triplicated section**, and there are a few genericity gaps for something labelled a template.

## A. Broken links / refs — these will actively mislead a reader

1. **README "Contributing" — 4 of 5 anchors are dead.** Lines 106/108/109/114 link into `CLAUDE.md` (i.e. AGENTS.md) at `#versioning`, `#commit-message-style`, `#commit-push-finalize-flow`, `#pre-commit-checklist`. None of those headings exist in AGENTS.md (I grepped — only `#code-conventions` at line 111 resolves). That content now lives in `notes/cycle-protocol.md` (`#numbering`, `#commit-description`, `#per-commit-flow`, `#pushing`). The section's premise ("canonical in CLAUDE.md") and its terminology ("Commit-Push-Finalize Flow", "two-checkpoint", "single-step vs multi-step") are stale relative to the cycle vocabulary the reorg adopted.

2. **`notes/todo.md` refs `[1]`/`[2]` point at the wrong path.** Lines 43-44 define them as `chores-01.md#…`, but the file moved to `notes/chores/chores-01.md`. From `todo.md` the path must be `chores/chores-01.md#…`. (The `#anchor` slugs themselves are correct.)

3. **README typo, line 29:** `git clone --recurisve` → `--recursive`. It's in a copy-paste command block.

4. **README orphan ref, line 134:** `[1]: https://github.com/karpathy/autoresearch` is defined but never cited (grep confirms only the definition line), and points at an unrelated repo. Looks like leftover — drop it or wire it up.

## B. Duplication (your stated goal)

5. **"Cross-repo Linking with Git Trailers" + the `ochid:` example block exists in three places:** AGENTS.md (canonical, lines 111+), README.md (84-99), and `notes/jj-tips.md` (343-362). The jj-tips.md copy is the most redundant — `ochid` is a *convention*, not a jj mechanic — collapse it to a one-line pointer. README's teaser can stay but needn't re-print the example block that AGENTS.md owns.

6. **`notes/chores/chores-01.md` references Prose form twice** — lines 3-7 link it, then line 9 redundantly repeats "See [Prose form]". Drop line 9.

7. *(minor)* The cargo cycle list is spelled out in both AGENTS.md's "Cycle Protocol" summary (line 475+) and `cycle-protocol.md`'s per-commit flow. Acceptable as summary-plus-pointer, but it's a maintenance seam — if the cycle changes, two spots need editing.

## C. Genericity — template-readiness gaps

8. **No `Cargo.toml` / `src/` exist yet** (confirmed), yet the protocol leans on `cargo fmt/clippy/test/install`, `Cargo.toml` version bumps, and `vc-x1 -V`. Fine as forward-looking scaffolding, but nothing tells a template adopter "these steps assume you've added the crate **and** installed the companion `vc-x1` tool." A short "Assumptions / Prerequisites" note would make the template honest about what it presumes.

9. **AGENTS.md "Chores commit references" example URL uses `winksaville/vc-x1`** (the tool's own repo), not `vc-template-x1` or an `<owner>/<repo>` placeholder — inconsistent for a template a reader is meant to copy.

10. **ARCHITECTURE.md overpromises.** Its intro says it "defines how this code is structured," but it's a See-also stub (and there's no code yet). Soften to "will define…" or mark it explicitly a stub.

## D. Consistency nits

11. **AGENTS.md link paths vary by file:** `../AGENTS.md` (notes), `/AGENTS.md` (todo.md:27, chores:9), `../../AGENTS.md` (chores:3), `AGENTS.md` (ARCHITECTURE.md). All resolve on GitHub, but one convention per directory depth is cleaner.

12. **The dummy worked-example violates the template's own exact-title rule.** todo.md Done "A completed dummy chore (20260322)" vs chores header "...(20260322 **0.1.0**)"; todo "A dummy chore **not completed**" vs chores "A dummy chore **(TBD)**". Since this *is* the template's demonstration of the "headings/Done/[N]-ref use exactly the commit title" convention, making them match would model the rule instead of breaking it.

---

If you want, I'd fix **A** first (the dead links are the real cost), then **B5/B6** for the dedup goal, and leave **C/D** to your call. Want me to apply those edits to the working copy now? Note there's no active cycle and the working copy has no description yet — I can either just edit in place, or set up a `## In Progress` cycle block first per the protocol. Which do you prefer?

# Review 1 — template genericity & de-duplication

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It records
a one-off review of the docs reorg that created `AGENTS.md` /
`ARCHITECTURE.md` and split the cycle protocol out of
`CLAUDE.md`, against two goals: a generic, template-ready file
set, and minimal duplication across files.

## In Progress

**Template genericity & de-duplication review**

Verdict: the de-duplication is mostly well done — the
`cargo --locked` gotcha was consolidated into `README.md` and the
layered `AGENTS.md` → `cycle-protocol.md` pointers read cleanly.
But the reorg left a batch of dangling links, one triplicated
section, and a few genericity gaps for a file set labelled a
template. Findings grouped below; the actionable fix list lives
in `## Todo`.

### A. Broken links / refs (will mislead a reader)

- README "Contributing" — 4 of 5 anchors are dead
  (README.md:104-114). Links to `CLAUDE.md#versioning`,
  `#commit-message-style`, `#commit-push-finalize-flow`, and
  `#pre-commit-checklist` have no matching heading in AGENTS.md
  (only `#code-conventions` resolves). The bot thinks the reorg
  moved that content to `notes/cycle-protocol.md` (`#numbering`,
  `#commit-description`, `#per-commit-flow`, `#pushing`) without
  repointing README. The "canonical in CLAUDE.md" premise and
  the terms ("Commit-Push-Finalize Flow", "two-checkpoint",
  "single-step vs multi-step") are stale against the cycle
  vocabulary.
- `notes/todo.md` refs `[1]`/`[2]` use the wrong path
  (notes/todo.md:43-44): `chores-01.md#…`, but the file moved to
  `notes/chores/chores-01.md`, so from todo.md the path is
  `chores/chores-01.md#…`. The `#anchor` slugs are correct.
- README typo (README.md:29): `git clone --recurisve` →
  `--recursive`, inside a copy-paste command block.
- README orphan ref (README.md:134):
  `[1]: https://github.com/karpathy/autoresearch` is defined but
  never cited and points at an unrelated repo. The bot thinks it
  is leftover — drop it or wire it up.

### B. Duplication (the stated goal)

- "Cross-repo Linking with Git Trailers" + the `ochid:` example
  block appears three times: AGENTS.md (canonical, 111+),
  README.md (84-99), `notes/jj-tips.md` (343-362). The jj-tips
  copy is the most redundant — `ochid` is a convention, not a jj
  mechanic — so collapse it to a one-line pointer; README's
  teaser can stay but needn't re-print the example block.
- `notes/chores/chores-01.md` references Prose form twice
  (lines 3-7, then 9) — drop the line-9 repeat.
- (minor) The cargo cycle list is spelled out in both the
  AGENTS.md "Cycle Protocol" summary (475+) and
  `cycle-protocol.md`'s per-commit flow — a two-spot maintenance
  seam, acceptable as summary-plus-pointer.

### C. Genericity (template-readiness)

- No `Cargo.toml` / `src/` exist yet, but the protocol leans on
  `cargo fmt/clippy/test/install`, `Cargo.toml` bumps, and
  `vc-x1 -V`. Nothing states that these steps assume an added
  crate plus an installed `vc-x1` companion tool — an
  "Assumptions / Prerequisites" note would make the template
  honest.
- AGENTS.md "Chores commit references" example URL uses
  `winksaville/vc-x1` (the tool's own repo), not an
  `<owner>/<repo>` placeholder — inconsistent for a template.
- ARCHITECTURE.md intro claims it "defines how this code is
  structured" but is a See-also stub with no code yet — soften
  to "will define…" or mark it a stub.

### D. Consistency nits

- AGENTS.md link paths vary by file: `../AGENTS.md` (notes),
  `/AGENTS.md` (todo.md:27, chores:9), `../../AGENTS.md`
  (chores:3), `AGENTS.md` (ARCHITECTURE.md). All resolve on
  GitHub; pick one convention per directory depth.
- The dummy worked-example breaks the template's own exact-title
  rule: todo Done "A completed dummy chore (20260322)" vs chores
  header "…(20260322 0.1.0)"; todo "A dummy chore not completed"
  vs chores "A dummy chore (TBD)". Making them match would model
  the rule the template teaches.

## Todo

1. Repoint README "Contributing" dead anchors to
   `cycle-protocol.md` and refresh the stale terminology
   (README.md:104-114).
2. Fix `notes/todo.md` refs `[1]`/`[2]` path: `chores-01.md` →
   `chores/chores-01.md` (notes/todo.md:43-44).
3. Fix README typo `--recurisve` → `--recursive` (README.md:29).
4. Drop (or wire up) README orphan ref `[1]` to
   karpathy/autoresearch (README.md:134).
5. De-dup the `ochid` section: collapse the jj-tips.md copy to a
   pointer and trim the README teaser
   (notes/jj-tips.md:343-362, README.md:84-99).
6. Drop the duplicate Prose-form reference in chores-01.md
   (notes/chores/chores-01.md:9).
7. Add an "Assumptions / Prerequisites" note (no `Cargo.toml` /
   `src` yet; `vc-x1` + cargo presumed).
8. Genericize the AGENTS.md chores-commit example URL
   (`winksaville/vc-x1` → `<owner>/<repo>` placeholder).
9. Soften the ARCHITECTURE.md intro or mark it a stub.
10. Unify AGENTS.md link-path style per directory depth.
11. Make the dummy worked-example titles satisfy the exact-title
    rule (todo Done / chores header / todo entry consistent).
12. (minor) Decide whether to single-source the cargo-cycle list
    now duplicated in AGENTS.md and cycle-protocol.md.

## Done

- Review completed.

# References

432	### Chores commit references
433	
434	The first line under a chores section header is a `Commits:`
435	line citing the git commit(s) that section records:
436	
437	```
438	## refactor: extract config loader (0.2.0)
439	
440	Commits: [[3]]
441	
442	<intro paragraph...>
443	```
444	
445	`Commits:` uses the file-local `[N]` reference machinery (see
446	[Reference numbering](#reference-numbering)),
447	**double-bracketed** so the brackets render — `Commits: [[3]]`,
448	or `Commits: [[3]],[[5]]` for several. (`[[3]]` shows as a
449	literal `[`, the `[3]` link, then a literal `]`; the inner
450	`[3]` resolves against its `[3]:` definition — CommonMark /
451	GitHub / VS Code all do this.) The `# References` definition
452	puts the **commit URL** as the destination, with the **full
453	40-hex SHA** in the title slot:
454	
455	```
456	[3]: https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1/commit/<12-hex> "<40-hex>"
457	```
458	
459	- The 12-hex short SHA in the URL keeps it short; GitHub /
460	  GitLab resolve a unique prefix to the canonical commit page
461	  (GitLab's path has a `/-/` before `commit/`).
462	- The full SHA in the title is host-agnostic and unambiguous —
463	  it survives a repo host change, `git show <40-hex>` works in
464	  any clone, and external tooling scraping the notes (a
465	  database, say) gets the canonical identifier.
466	
467	**Timing.** The commit doesn't exist when its chores section is
468	written, so the `Commits:` line is **backfilled when the next
469	change to that repo is started** — the cycle-start step grabs
470	the just-pushed commit's URL + SHA and fills it in. The single
471	newest section is briefly `Commits:`-less; that's fine — the
472	commit itself is the record, and `git log --grep "(X.Y.Z)"`
473	finds it.
474	
475	## Cycle Protocol
476	
477	Every change runs as a **cycle**: Preparation (`X.Y.Z-0`) →
478	Work commits (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, …) → Close-out (bare
479	`X.Y.Z`). Each commit runs through a per-commit flow whose
480	cargo cycle is `cargo fmt`, `cargo clippy --all-targets
481	-- -D warnings`, `cargo test`, `cargo install --path .
482	--locked` (skip-able for purely-docs commits; mandatory at
483	close-out). The full protocol — numbering, per-commit
484	flow, reviewing changes, close-out, pushing, ochid
485	trailers, sub-cycles — lives in
486	[`notes/cycle-protocol.md`](notes/cycle-protocol.md). Read
487	it before any commit work.
488	
489	
490	## Code Conventions

file.content:
### Chores commit references

The first line under a chores section header is a `Commits:`
line citing the git commit(s) that section records:

```
## refactor: extract config loader (0.2.0)

Commits: [[3]]

<intro paragraph...>
```

`Commits:` uses the file-local `[N]` reference machinery (see
[Reference numbering](#reference-numbering)),
**double-bracketed** so the brackets render — `Commits: [[3]]`,
or `Commits: [[3]],[[5]]` for several. (`[[3]]` shows as a
literal `[`, the `[3]` link, then a literal `]`; the inner
`[3]` resolves against its `[3]:` definition — CommonMark /
GitHub / VS Code all do this.) The `# References` definition
puts the **commit URL** as the destination, with the **full
40-hex SHA** in the title slot:

```
[3]: https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1/commit/<12-hex> "<40-hex>"
```

- The 12-hex short SHA in the URL keeps it short; GitHub /
  GitLab resolve a unique prefix to the canonical commit page
  (GitLab's path has a `/-/` before `commit/`).
- The full SHA in the title is host-agnostic and unambiguous —
  it survives a repo host change, `git show <40-hex>` works in
  any clone, and external tooling scraping the notes (a
  database, say) gets the canonical identifier.

**Timing.** The commit doesn't exist when its chores section is
written, so the `Commits:` line is **backfilled when the next
change to that repo is started** — the cycle-start step grabs
the just-pushed commit's URL + SHA and fills it in. The single
newest section is briefly `Commits:`-less; that's fine — the
commit itself is the record, and `git log --grep "(X.Y.Z)"`
finds it.

## Cycle Protocol

Every change runs as a **cycle**: Preparation (`X.Y.Z-0`) →
Work commits (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, …) → Close-out (bare
`X.Y.Z`). Each commit runs through a per-commit flow whose
cargo cycle is `cargo fmt`, `cargo clippy --all-targets
-- -D warnings`, `cargo test`, `cargo install --path .
--locked` (skip-able for purely-docs commits; mandatory at
close-out). The full protocol — numbering, per-commit
flow, reviewing changes, close-out, pushing, ochid
trailers, sub-cycles — lives in
[`notes/cycle-protocol.md`](notes/cycle-protocol.md). Read
it before any commit work.


## Code Conventions

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# vc-template-x1

This is the main repo of a dual-repo convention for using
a bot to help in the development of a coding project. The goal
is that this main repo contains the "what", while the partner
bot repo contains "why" and "how". The key to the convention
is each change is cross-referenced to the other. Thus there
is a coherent story of the development of the project across time.

The beginnings of that tool is [vc-x1](https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1)
which currently does achieve this goal, but is being used as a
first test bed.

## This is a template — customize freely

`CLAUDE.md` and this `README.md` are just starting points.
Read both and edit either to match the conventions you want for your
project. In particular, decisions like the `cargo install` behavior
(default re-resolve vs `--locked`) under [Releasing](#releasing) are
yours to make — pick what fits and update the docs (and the
per-commit cargo cycle) so the choice is recorded for future readers.

## Cloning

The Bot session repo is setup as a git submodule which
makes it easier to clone both repos. The easiest is to
clone both when doing the initial clone:
```
git clone --recurisve git@github.com:winksaville/vc-template-x1
```

If you forget to use --recursive you need to do two additional
steps "init" and "update":
```
git clone git@github.com:winksaville/vc-template-x1
git submodule init
git submodule update
```

And these can be combined using 'update --init' if you like
```
git clone git@github.com:winksaville/vc-template-x1
git submodule update --init
```

## Releasing

**`cargo install` lockfile gotcha.** If you install your project's
binary with `cargo install --path .`, also pass `--locked`. By
default `cargo install` ignores `Cargo.lock` and re-resolves
dependencies from scratch, so it can silently build the binary from
a different — possibly broken — dependency graph than `cargo build`
/ `cargo test` ran against.

| command | reads `Cargo.lock`? | re-resolves? |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `cargo build` / `test` / `run`  | yes     | only if `Cargo.toml` changed |
| `cargo build --locked` (etc.)   | yes     | never; errors if it would need to |
| `cargo install` (default)       | **no**  | always, from scratch |
| `cargo install --locked`        | yes     | never; errors if it would need to |

There's no stable cargo config knob to make `--locked` the default
for `cargo install`, so the discipline lives in the per-commit cargo
cycle and shell aliases (e.g. `alias ci='cargo install --locked'`).

The trade-off is predictable-as-tested (`--locked`) vs picking up
upstream fixes via fresh resolves (default). Either is defensible —
pick what fits your project, then edit this section and the
per-commit cargo cycle to match. Background:
[cargo#7169](https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/issues/7169),
[cargo#9436](https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/issues/9436).

## jj Tips for Git Users

This project uses [Jujutsu (jj)](https://docs.jj-vcs.dev/latest/)
alongside git. New to jj? See
[Steve Klabnik](https://github.com/steveklabnik)'s
[Jujutsu tutorial](https://steveklabnik.github.io/jujutsu-tutorial).

Repo-specific how-tos — initial commit, pushing, modifying and
force-pushing a commit, revsets, and a useful-commands reference —
live in [notes/jj-tips.md](notes/jj-tips.md).

## Cross-repo Linking with Git Trailers

Commits in each repo use [git trailers](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-interpret-trailers)
to cross-reference their counterpart in the other repo. The `ochid`
(Other Change ID) trailer contains a workspace-root-relative path
and jj changeID:

```
ochid: /.claude/xvzvruqo   # points to a .claude repo change
ochid: /wtpmottv            # points to an app repo change
```

This cross-reference is the defining mechanism of the dual-repo
convention. For the full definition — path syntax, per-commit
mechanics, and `.vc-config.toml` — see
[Cross-repo linking (ochid trailers)](AGENTS.md#cross-repo-linking-ochid-trailers).

## Contributing

Bot-following workflow, commit conventions, and code style are
canonical in [CLAUDE.md](CLAUDE.md):

- [Versioning during development](CLAUDE.md#versioning) — `-N`
  pre-release suffix convention (single-step vs multi-step).
- [Commit message style](CLAUDE.md#commit-message-style).
- [Commit-Push-Finalize Flow](CLAUDE.md#commit-push-finalize-flow) —
  two-checkpoint per-step discipline.
- [Code Conventions](CLAUDE.md#code-conventions) — doc comments on
  every file / fn / method, `// OK: …` on `unwrap*` calls,
  ask-on-ambiguity, stuck detection.
- [Pre-commit checklist](CLAUDE.md#pre-commit-checklist).

Task tracking and release details live under [notes/](notes/):
near-term tasks in [notes/todo.md](notes/todo.md), per-release
details in `notes/chores-*.md`, and notes-specific formatting
rules in [notes/README.md](notes/README.md).

## License

Licensed under either of

- Apache License, Version 2.0 ([LICENSE-APACHE](LICENSE-APACHE) or http://apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0)
- MIT license ([LICENSE-MIT](LICENSE-MIT) or http://opensource.org/licenses/MIT)

### Contribution

Unless you explicitly state otherwise, any contribution intentionally submitted
for inclusion in the work by you, as defined in the Apache-2.0 license, shall
be dual licensed as above, without any additional terms or conditions.

[1]: https://github.com/karpathy/autoresearch

# vc-template-x1

This is the main repo of a dual-repo convention for using
a bot to help in the development of a coding project. The goal
is that this main repo contains the "what", while the partner
bot repo contains "why" and "how". The key to the convention
is each change is cross-referenced to the other. Thus there
is a coherent story of the development of the project across time.

The beginnings of that tool is [vc-x1](https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1)
which currently does achieve this goal, but is being used as a
first test bed.

## This is a template — customize freely

`CLAUDE.md` and this `README.md` are just starting points.
Read both and edit either to match the conventions you want for your
project. In particular, decisions like the `cargo install` behavior
(default re-resolve vs `--locked`) under [Releasing](#releasing) are
yours to make — pick what fits and update the docs (and the
per-commit cargo cycle) so the choice is recorded for future readers.

## Assumptions

The workflow documented here presumes two things this template
does not itself ship:

- **A Rust crate.** The per-commit cargo cycle (`cargo fmt` /
  `clippy` / `test` / `install`) and the `Cargo.toml` version
  bumps assume you have added a crate; the template carries docs
  and notes only (no `Cargo.toml` / `src/` yet).
- **The `vc-x1` companion tool.** Steps that invoke `vc-x1`
  (`fix-todo`, `validate-todo`, `chid`, `push`, `finalize`) need
  [vc-x1](https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1) installed; the
  underlying jj steps are documented so the flow also works
  without it.

## Cloning

The Bot session repo is setup as a git submodule which
makes it easier to clone both repos. The easiest is to
clone both when doing the initial clone:
```
git clone --recursive git@github.com:winksaville/vc-template-x1
```

If you forget to use --recursive you need to do two additional
steps "init" and "update":
```
git clone git@github.com:winksaville/vc-template-x1
git submodule init
git submodule update
```

And these can be combined using 'update --init' if you like
```
git clone git@github.com:winksaville/vc-template-x1
git submodule update --init
```

## Releasing

**`cargo install` lockfile gotcha.** If you install your project's
binary with `cargo install --path .`, also pass `--locked`. By
default `cargo install` ignores `Cargo.lock` and re-resolves
dependencies from scratch, so it can silently build the binary from
a different — possibly broken — dependency graph than `cargo build`
/ `cargo test` ran against.

| command | reads `Cargo.lock`? | re-resolves? |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `cargo build` / `test` / `run`  | yes     | only if `Cargo.toml` changed |
| `cargo build --locked` (etc.)   | yes     | never; errors if it would need to |
| `cargo install` (default)       | **no**  | always, from scratch |
| `cargo install --locked`        | yes     | never; errors if it would need to |

There's no stable cargo config knob to make `--locked` the default
for `cargo install`, so the discipline lives in the per-commit cargo
cycle and shell aliases (e.g. `alias ci='cargo install --locked'`).

The trade-off is predictable-as-tested (`--locked`) vs picking up
upstream fixes via fresh resolves (default). Either is defensible —
pick what fits your project, then edit this section and the
per-commit cargo cycle to match. Background:
[cargo#7169](https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/issues/7169),
[cargo#9436](https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/issues/9436).

## jj Tips for Git Users

This project uses [Jujutsu (jj)](https://docs.jj-vcs.dev/latest/)
alongside git. New to jj? See
[Steve Klabnik](https://github.com/steveklabnik)'s
[Jujutsu tutorial](https://steveklabnik.github.io/jujutsu-tutorial).

Repo-specific how-tos — initial commit, pushing, modifying and
force-pushing a commit, revsets, and a useful-commands reference —
live in [notes/jj-tips.md](notes/jj-tips.md).

## Cross-repo Linking with Git Trailers

Commits in each repo use [git trailers](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-interpret-trailers)
to cross-reference their counterpart in the other repo. The `ochid`
(Other Change ID) trailer contains a workspace-root-relative path
and jj changeID:

```
ochid: /.claude/xvzvruqo   # points to a .claude repo change
ochid: /wtpmottv            # points to an app repo change
```

This cross-reference is the defining mechanism of the dual-repo
convention. For the full definition — path syntax, per-commit
mechanics, and `.vc-config.toml` — see
[Cross-repo linking (ochid trailers)](AGENTS.md#cross-repo-linking-ochid-trailers).

## Contributing

Bot-following workflow, commit conventions, and code style are
canonical in [CLAUDE.md](CLAUDE.md):

- [Versioning during development](CLAUDE.md#versioning) — `-N`
  pre-release suffix convention (single-step vs multi-step).
- [Commit message style](CLAUDE.md#commit-message-style).
- [Commit-Push-Finalize Flow](CLAUDE.md#commit-push-finalize-flow) —
  two-checkpoint per-step discipline.
- [Code Conventions](CLAUDE.md#code-conventions) — doc comments on
  every file / fn / method, `// OK: …` on `unwrap*` calls,
  ask-on-ambiguity, stuck detection.
- [Pre-commit checklist](CLAUDE.md#pre-commit-checklist).

Task tracking and release details live under [notes/](notes/):
near-term tasks in [notes/todo.md](notes/todo.md), per-release
details in `notes/chores-*.md`, and notes-specific formatting
rules in [notes/README.md](notes/README.md).

## License

Licensed under either of

- Apache License, Version 2.0 ([LICENSE-APACHE](LICENSE-APACHE) or http://apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0)
- MIT license ([LICENSE-MIT](LICENSE-MIT) or http://opensource.org/licenses/MIT)

### Contribution

Unless you explicitly state otherwise, any contribution intentionally submitted
for inclusion in the work by you, as defined in the Apache-2.0 license, shall
be dual licensed as above, without any additional terms or conditions.

[1]: https://github.com/karpathy/autoresearch

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

When a `## Todo` item is picked up, its text moves here: the
problem overview and its list of things to do. That is followed
by the "plan" — a bulleted list of the development "ladder":
   - 0.xx.y-0 blah (done)
   - 0.xx.y-1 blah blah (current)
   - 0.xx.y-2 blah blah blah
   - 0.xx.y close-out and validation

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](/AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).


- A dummy chore not completed [[2]]

## Done

Completed tasks are moved from `## Todo` to here, `## Done`, as they are completed
and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.

- Unified doc link paths to relative (`../AGENTS.md`,
  `../todo.md`); removed the `/`-absolute outliers.
- Made the dummy worked-example titles satisfy the exact-title

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Review 1 — template genericity & de-duplication

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It records
a one-off review of the docs reorg that created `AGENTS.md` /
`ARCHITECTURE.md` and split the cycle protocol out of
`CLAUDE.md`, against two goals: a generic, template-ready file
set, and minimal duplication across files.

## In Progress

**Template genericity & de-duplication review**

Verdict: the de-duplication is mostly well done — the
`cargo --locked` gotcha was consolidated into `README.md` and the
layered `AGENTS.md` → `cycle-protocol.md` pointers read cleanly.
But the reorg left a batch of dangling links, one triplicated
section, and a few genericity gaps for a file set labelled a
template. Findings grouped below; the actionable fix list lives
in `## Todo`.

### A. Broken links / refs (will mislead a reader)

- README "Contributing" — 4 of 5 anchors are dead
  (README.md:104-114). Links to `CLAUDE.md#versioning`,
  `#commit-message-style`, `#commit-push-finalize-flow`, and
  `#pre-commit-checklist` have no matching heading in AGENTS.md
  (only `#code-conventions` resolves). The bot thinks the reorg
  moved that content to `notes/cycle-protocol.md` (`#numbering`,
  `#commit-description`, `#per-commit-flow`, `#pushing`) without
  repointing README. The "canonical in CLAUDE.md" premise and
  the terms ("Commit-Push-Finalize Flow", "two-checkpoint",
  "single-step vs multi-step") are stale against the cycle
  vocabulary.
- `notes/todo.md` refs `[1]`/`[2]` use the wrong path
  (notes/todo.md:43-44): `chores-01.md#…`, but the file moved to
  `notes/chores/chores-01.md`, so from todo.md the path is
  `chores/chores-01.md#…`. The `#anchor` slugs are correct.
- README typo (README.md:29): `git clone --recurisve` →
  `--recursive`, inside a copy-paste command block.
- README orphan ref (README.md:134):
  `[1]: https://github.com/karpathy/autoresearch` is defined but
  never cited and points at an unrelated repo. The bot thinks it
  is leftover — drop it or wire it up.

### B. Duplication (the stated goal)

- "Cross-repo Linking with Git Trailers" + the `ochid:` example
  block appears three times: AGENTS.md (canonical, 111+),
  README.md (84-99), `notes/jj-tips.md` (343-362). The jj-tips
  copy is the most redundant — `ochid` is a convention, not a jj
  mechanic — so collapse it to a one-line pointer; README's
  teaser can stay but needn't re-print the example block.
- `notes/chores/chores-01.md` references Prose form twice
  (lines 3-7, then 9) — drop the line-9 repeat.
- (minor) The cargo cycle list is spelled out in both the
  AGENTS.md "Cycle Protocol" summary (475+) and
  `cycle-protocol.md`'s per-commit flow — a two-spot maintenance
  seam, acceptable as summary-plus-pointer.

### C. Genericity (template-readiness)

- No `Cargo.toml` / `src/` exist yet, but the protocol leans on
  `cargo fmt/clippy/test/install`, `Cargo.toml` bumps, and
  `vc-x1 -V`. Nothing states that these steps assume an added
  crate plus an installed `vc-x1` companion tool — an
  "Assumptions / Prerequisites" note would make the template
  honest.
- AGENTS.md "Chores commit references" example URL uses
  `winksaville/vc-x1` (the tool's own repo), not an
  `<owner>/<repo>` placeholder — inconsistent for a template.
- ARCHITECTURE.md intro claims it "defines how this code is
  structured" but is a See-also stub with no code yet — soften
  to "will define…" or mark it a stub.

### D. Consistency nits

- AGENTS.md link paths vary by file: `../AGENTS.md` (notes),
  `/AGENTS.md` (todo.md:27, chores:9), `../../AGENTS.md`
  (chores:3), `AGENTS.md` (ARCHITECTURE.md). All resolve on
  GitHub; pick one convention per directory depth.
- The dummy worked-example breaks the template's own exact-title
  rule: todo Done "A completed dummy chore (20260322)" vs chores
  header "…(20260322 0.1.0)"; todo "A dummy chore not completed"
  vs chores "A dummy chore (TBD)". Making them match would model
  the rule the template teaches.

## Todo

1. Repoint README "Contributing" dead anchors to
   `cycle-protocol.md` and refresh the stale terminology
   (README.md:104-114).
2. Fix `notes/todo.md` refs `[1]`/`[2]` path: `chores-01.md` →
   `chores/chores-01.md` (notes/todo.md:43-44).
3. Fix README typo `--recurisve` → `--recursive` (README.md:29).
4. Drop (or wire up) README orphan ref `[1]` to
   karpathy/autoresearch (README.md:134).
5. De-dup the `ochid` section: collapse the jj-tips.md copy to a
   pointer and trim the README teaser
   (notes/jj-tips.md:343-362, README.md:84-99).
6. Drop the duplicate Prose-form reference in chores-01.md
   (notes/chores/chores-01.md:9).
7. Add an "Assumptions / Prerequisites" note (no `Cargo.toml` /
   `src` yet; `vc-x1` + cargo presumed).
8. Genericize the AGENTS.md chores-commit example URL
   (`winksaville/vc-x1` → `<owner>/<repo>` placeholder).
9. Soften the ARCHITECTURE.md intro or mark it a stub.
10. Unify AGENTS.md link-path style per directory depth.
11. Make the dummy worked-example titles satisfy the exact-title
    rule (todo Done / chores header / todo entry consistent).
12. (minor) Decide whether to single-source the cargo-cycle list
    now duplicated in AGENTS.md and cycle-protocol.md.

## Done

- Review completed.

What would you like to clarify? A few things that might help me frame this better:

- **What's your definition of "generic" here** — files with no `vc-template-x1`/`winksaville`/`vc-x1`-specific content, vs. the full template scaffolding a new project would adopt (which includes README/config that you'd then customize)?
- **Should `README.md` be in scope?** It names this repo throughout, but the template itself calls it a "starting point" — so it could count either way.
- **Licenses, `.gitignore`, `.vc-config.toml`** — bootstrap files for a new project, or out of scope since they're not the convention docs?
- **`notes/review1.md`** — I'm assuming exclude (it's a one-off review of this repo). Correct?

Or if you already have a specific list in mind, just give it to me and I'll build the script around it.

message.content: Have the list at the top of the script one line per file so it's easy to modify, have it contiain "all" generic files including licenses

content.text: Got it — full generic set including licenses, list at the top, one path per line. Writing it now.

input.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# copy-generic.sh — copy this template's generic files into another
# directory, recreating each file's relative path and making the
# destination directories as needed.
#
# Usage: ./copy-generic.sh <dest-dir>
#
# Edit the FILES list below to change what gets copied: one path per
# line, relative to the repo root. A listed path that doesn't exist
# is reported and skipped (the rest still copy). Existing files at
# the destination are overwritten.

set -euo pipefail

# Generic template files — one per line, repo-root-relative.
FILES=(
  AGENTS.md
  CLAUDE.md
  ARCHITECTURE.md
  README.md
  LICENSE-APACHE
  LICENSE-MIT
  .gitignore
  .vc-config.toml
  notes/README.md
  notes/todo.md
  notes/todo-backlog.md
  notes/bugs.md
  notes/done.md
  notes/cycle-protocol.md
  notes/jj-tips.md
  notes/substep-test.sh
  notes/chores/chores-01.md
)

# Repo root = the directory this script lives in, so it works
# regardless of the caller's current working directory.
SRC_ROOT="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)"

usage() {
  echo "Usage: ${0##*/} <dest-dir>" >&2
  exit 2
}

[ $# -eq 1 ] || usage
DEST_ROOT="$1"

copied=0
missing=0
for rel in "${FILES[@]}"; do
  src="$SRC_ROOT/$rel"
  dest="$DEST_ROOT/$rel"
  if [ ! -f "$src" ]; then
    echo "skip (missing): $rel" >&2
    missing=$((missing + 1))
    continue
  fi
  mkdir -p "$(dirname "$dest")"
  cp -p "$src" "$dest"
  echo "copied: $rel"
  copied=$((copied + 1))
done

echo "Done: $copied copied, $missing missing -> $DEST_ROOT"

content.text:
`copy-generic.sh` is in the repo root, executable, and verified.

**What it does:**
- `FILES=( … )` array at the top — one repo-root-relative path per line, easy to add/remove.
- Includes all generic template files: the four root docs (`AGENTS.md`, `CLAUDE.md`, `ARCHITECTURE.md`, `README.md`), **both licenses**, `.gitignore`, `.vc-config.toml`, and the full `notes/` tree (minus `review1.md`, the one-off review of this repo).
- `Usage: ./copy-generic.sh <dest-dir>` — `mkdir -p` recreates each file's relative path under the destination, so subdirs like `notes/chores/` appear as needed.
- `cp -p` preserves mode/timestamps (keeps `substep-test.sh` executable); resolves its own location so it runs from any cwd; reports per-file `copied`/`skip (missing)` and a final tally.

**Deliberately excluded** (easy to add a line if you want them): `notes/review1.md` (repo-specific), `.gitmodules` (points at this project's specific submodule), and the live `.git`/`.jj`/`.claude`/`.vc-x1` dirs.

Nothing committed. Want me to add the script to the `FILES` list itself (so a new project gets the copier too), or leave it out?

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# vc-template-x1

This is the main repo of a dual-repo convention for using
a bot to help in the development of a coding project. The goal
is that this main repo contains the "what", while the partner
bot repo contains "why" and "how". The key to the convention
is each change is cross-referenced to the other. Thus there
is a coherent story of the development of the project across time.

The beginnings of that tool is [vc-x1](https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1)
which currently does achieve this goal, but is being used as a
first test bed.

## This is a template — customize freely

`CLAUDE.md` and this `README.md` are just starting points.
Read both and edit either to match the conventions you want for your
project. In particular, decisions like the `cargo install` behavior
(default re-resolve vs `--locked`) under [Releasing](#releasing) are
yours to make — pick what fits and update the docs (and the
per-commit cargo cycle) so the choice is recorded for future readers.

## Assumptions

The workflow documented here presumes two things this template
does not itself ship:

- **A Rust crate.** The per-commit cargo cycle (`cargo fmt` /
  `clippy` / `test` / `install`) and the `Cargo.toml` version
  bumps assume you have added a crate; the template carries docs
  and notes only (no `Cargo.toml` / `src/` yet).
- **The `vc-x1` companion tool.** Steps that invoke `vc-x1`
  (`fix-todo`, `validate-todo`, `chid`, `push`, `finalize`) need
  [vc-x1](https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1) installed; the
  underlying jj steps are documented so the flow also works
  without it.

## Cloning

The Bot session repo is setup as a git submodule which
makes it easier to clone both repos. The easiest is to
clone both when doing the initial clone:
```
git clone --recursive git@github.com:winksaville/vc-template-x1
```

If you forget to use --recursive you need to do two additional
steps "init" and "update":
```
git clone git@github.com:winksaville/vc-template-x1
git submodule init
git submodule update
```

And these can be combined using 'update --init' if you like
```
git clone git@github.com:winksaville/vc-template-x1
git submodule update --init
```

## Releasing

**`cargo install` lockfile gotcha.** If you install your project's
binary with `cargo install --path .`, also pass `--locked`. By
default `cargo install` ignores `Cargo.lock` and re-resolves
dependencies from scratch, so it can silently build the binary from
a different — possibly broken — dependency graph than `cargo build`
/ `cargo test` ran against.

| command | reads `Cargo.lock`? | re-resolves? |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `cargo build` / `test` / `run`  | yes     | only if `Cargo.toml` changed |
| `cargo build --locked` (etc.)   | yes     | never; errors if it would need to |
| `cargo install` (default)       | **no**  | always, from scratch |
| `cargo install --locked`        | yes     | never; errors if it would need to |

There's no stable cargo config knob to make `--locked` the default
for `cargo install`, so the discipline lives in the per-commit cargo
cycle and shell aliases (e.g. `alias ci='cargo install --locked'`).

The trade-off is predictable-as-tested (`--locked`) vs picking up
upstream fixes via fresh resolves (default). Either is defensible —
pick what fits your project, then edit this section and the
per-commit cargo cycle to match. Background:
[cargo#7169](https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/issues/7169),
[cargo#9436](https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/issues/9436).

## jj Tips for Git Users

This project uses [Jujutsu (jj)](https://docs.jj-vcs.dev/latest/)
alongside git. New to jj? See
[Steve Klabnik](https://github.com/steveklabnik)'s
[Jujutsu tutorial](https://steveklabnik.github.io/jujutsu-tutorial).

Repo-specific how-tos — initial commit, pushing, modifying and
force-pushing a commit, revsets, and a useful-commands reference —
live in [notes/jj-tips.md](notes/jj-tips.md).

## Cross-repo Linking with Git Trailers

Commits in each repo use [git trailers](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-interpret-trailers)
to cross-reference their counterpart in the other repo via an
`ochid` (Other Change ID) trailer — the defining mechanism of the
dual-repo convention. For the full definition — trailer syntax,
the example shape, per-commit mechanics, and `.vc-config.toml` —
see
[Cross-repo linking (ochid trailers)](AGENTS.md#cross-repo-linking-ochid-trailers).

## Contributing

Bot-following workflow, commit conventions, and code style are
canonical in [AGENTS.md](AGENTS.md) (which `CLAUDE.md` imports)
and [notes/cycle-protocol.md](notes/cycle-protocol.md):

- [Cycle numbering](notes/cycle-protocol.md#numbering) — the
  `X.Y.Z-N` phase-suffix convention (Preparation / Work /
  Close-out).
- [Commit description](notes/cycle-protocol.md#commit-description)
  — Conventional Commits + `(version)` suffix and per-repo body
  shape.
- [Per-commit flow](notes/cycle-protocol.md#per-commit-flow) —
  the cargo cycle plus the Work and Commit-Description review
  gates.
- [Code Conventions](AGENTS.md#code-conventions) — doc comments on
  every file / fn / method, `// OK: …` on `unwrap*` calls,
  ask-on-ambiguity, stuck detection.

Task tracking and release details live under [notes/](notes/):
near-term tasks in [notes/todo.md](notes/todo.md), per-release
details in `notes/chores/chores-*.md`, and notes-specific
formatting rules in [notes/README.md](notes/README.md).

## License

Licensed under either of

- Apache License, Version 2.0 ([LICENSE-APACHE](LICENSE-APACHE) or http://apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0)
- MIT license ([LICENSE-MIT](LICENSE-MIT) or http://opensource.org/licenses/MIT)

### Contribution

Unless you explicitly state otherwise, any contribution intentionally submitted
for inclusion in the work by you, as defined in the Apache-2.0 license, shall
be dual licensed as above, without any additional terms or conditions.

message.content: I moved it, didn't want that checked in. I also change .gitignore to ignore .claude, it should not be commited, thoughts?

content.text:
Yes — ignoring `.claude` in the app repo is the right call. My reasoning:

- **It's a separate repo, not app content.** `.claude` is the bot session repo with its own jj-git history, cross-linked to the app repo only via `ochid` trailers (per AGENTS.md). The app repo was never supposed to track its *contents* — the deleted submodule was the old "reference but don't absorb" mechanism, and a gitignore is the cleaner replacement now that it's a plain repo.
- **It's churning session data.** `.claude` holds continuously-rewritten session `.jsonl` files — we already hit jj's 1 MiB snapshot refusal on one of them. Keeping it out of the app repo avoids that noise entirely.
- **Mental model stays intact:** "should not be committed" means *not in the app repo*. `.claude` still gets committed and pushed — in its own repo (`-R .claude`). So nothing is lost.

Two suggestions:

1. **Anchor the pattern** to `/.claude/` (root-anchored, trailing slash) rather than bare `.claude`, so it ignores only the top-level bot repo and not any incidental `.claude` deeper in a tree.
2. **Check for a leftover gitlink.** A submodule leaves a gitlink entry in git's index; with `.gitmodules` now gone, make sure there isn't a dangling `.claude` submodule pointer still staged. (I'd verify with a quick `git ls-files .claude` / `jj st`, but I'll hold since you've been steering each step.)

A small doc follow-up worth noting later: AGENTS.md describes `.claude` as a symlinked sub-repo but doesn't mention it's gitignored by the app repo — worth a line once this settles.

For the commit you asked for: scope has changed since "commit this" (review1.md moved out, `.gitignore` now ignores `.claude`, `.gitmodules` deleted). Want me to verify the final working-copy scope and show you the proposed commit title + body before I run `jj commit`?

content.text:
Honestly — I didn't confirm it the way the convention calls for. Here's exactly what I did:

I ran `jj log -R .claude -r @ -T 'change_id.shortest(8)'`, which returned `ppmwxuno` — that's just the **current `.claude` working-copy `@`** (the in-progress, undescribed session commit). I assumed it's the counterpart for this app commit and dropped it straight into the trailer.

What that skipped:
- **AGENTS.md prescribes `vc-x1 chid -s code,bot -L`** to capture the pair (first line app, second `.claude`) — I didn't use it.
- I didn't actually verify `ppmwxuno` is the right `.claude` change to pair with. It's the live working copy; the convention's logic is that its chid stays stable across snapshots/finalize so the trailer resolves — but I took that on faith rather than confirming.
- And ochid resolvability is local-only (the chid isn't stored in the repo), so "confirm" really means confirming it's the intended counterpart, which I didn't do.

So "current .claude change id (for ochid)" overstated it — I grabbed a plausible value, I didn't confirm it. Good catch, and nothing was committed since you stopped it.

How would you like me to get it right? Options:
- Run `vc-x1 chid -s code,bot -L` (if `vc-x1` is installed here) and use what it reports.
- Or you tell me the correct `.claude` counterpart chid.
- Or commit the app repo **without** the `ochid:` trailer for now and add it at push/finalize time (when the `.claude` commit is actually paired), which seems closer to how the cadence is meant to work.

Which do you prefer?

content.text:
Clone succeeded, and the comparison is revealing:

**They match where it counts — and change IDs survive the clone.**

| | published `main` tip | working copy `@` |
|---|---|---|
| `/tmp` fresh clone | `ortvnxqm` / `c13df436` | `pmslxqtu` (fresh empty) |
| `../vc-template-x1.claude` | `ortvnxqm` / `c13df436` | `ypqntykx` |

- The **pushed commit** `c13df436` ("docs: rework CLAUDE.md as canonical primary copy") carries the **same change ID `ortvnqm` in both clones**. So jj change IDs *do* travel with the commit (jj stores `change-id` in the git commit header) — they survive `jj git clone`/fetch.
- Only the **local working-copy `@`** differs (`pmslxqtu` vs `ypqntykx`) — each clone mints a fresh empty `@` with its own random chid. That's expected and local-only.

**What this means for my ochid:**
- `ochid: /.claude/ppmwxuno` was wrong in the way that matters. `ppmwxuno` is *this machine's* local, **unpushed** `.claude` working copy — it exists in no clone and resolves nowhere. I pointed the trailer at an ephemeral local commit.
- A correct ochid must point at a **pushed** `.claude` change (like the prior app commit's `ochid: /.claude/ortvnqm`, which is the published counterpart and resolves in both clones above). The pairing is meant to be fixed at **push/finalize**, once this session's `.claude` work is actually committed and pushed — not grabbed from the live working copy beforehand.

So the clean path is: **commit the app repo now without the `ochid:` trailer**, and add it at push/finalize when the `.claude` counterpart is real and clonable.

**Also — a doc bug found:** AGENTS.md's *Resolvability* section claims "jj changeIDs are generated at `jj git init` time and are not stored in the repo, so ochid references resolve only for tools with access to the local jj repo." This experiment contradicts that for pushed commits — `ortvnqm` resolved identically in a brand-new clone. Worth correcting (the true caveat is narrower: only *unpushed working-copy* chids are local-only).

I left the clone at `/tmp/vc-template-x1.claude`. Want me to (a) re-commit the app repo without the ochid trailer, (b) fix the AGENTS.md Resolvability section, or both?

111	## Cross-repo linking (ochid trailers)
112	
113	The cross-reference between the app repo and the `.claude` bot
114	repo is what makes the dual-repo work: every commit points at its
115	counterpart in the other repo, so the "what" (code) and the
116	"why / how" (session) stay linked across time. That pointer is the
117	**ochid** (Other Change ID) git trailer.
118	
119	A **chid** is jj's change ID — a permanent identifier that
120	survives rebases and `describe`s (unlike the commit ID / git SHA,
121	which changes on rewrite). An **ochid** trailer carries the
122	counterpart commit's chid as a workspace-root-relative path:
123	
124	- Paths start with `/` — the workspace root, i.e. the app repo
125	  (vc-template-x1). `/.claude` is the bot session sub-repo.
126	- `ochid: /<chid>` references a change in the **app repo**.
127	- `ochid: /.claude/<chid>` references a change in the **`.claude`
128	  repo**.
129	
130	Trailers are blank-line-separated `key: value` lines at the end of
131	the commit body:
132	
133	```
134	ochid: /.claude/xvzvruqo   # points to a .claude repo change
135	ochid: /wtpmottv           # points to an app repo change
136	```
137

139	
140	- **Code-side commits** each carry one
141	  `ochid: /.claude/<.claude-chid>` — the `.claude` change ID.
142	- **The `.claude` commit** carries one `ochid: /<code-chid>` per
143	  code commit in that push. More than one occurs on a Merge
144	  non-ff close-out (one ochid per Work commit in the cycle).
145	
146	Use `vc-x1 chid -s code,bot -L` to capture the change IDs (first
147	line app, second `.claude`).
148	
149	### Resolvability
150	
151	jj changeIDs are generated at `jj git init` time and are not
152	stored in the repo, so ochid references resolve only for tools
153	with access to the local jj repo (e.g. vc-x1).
154	
155	### .vc-config.toml
156	
157	Each repo contains a `.vc-config.toml` that identifies its
158	location within the workspace, so tools resolve these paths
159	without repeating the workspace path in every trailer:
160	
161	```toml
162	# In vc-template-x1 (workspace root):
163	[workspace]
164	path = "/"
165	
166	# In .claude (sub-repo):

file.content:
## Cross-repo linking (ochid trailers)

The cross-reference between the app repo and the `.claude` bot
repo is what makes the dual-repo work: every commit points at its
counterpart in the other repo, so the "what" (code) and the
"why / how" (session) stay linked across time. That pointer is the
**ochid** (Other Change ID) git trailer.

A **chid** is jj's change ID — a permanent identifier that
survives rebases and `describe`s (unlike the commit ID / git SHA,
which changes on rewrite). An **ochid** trailer carries the
counterpart commit's chid as a workspace-root-relative path:

- Paths start with `/` — the workspace root, i.e. the app repo
  (vc-template-x1). `/.claude` is the bot session sub-repo.
- `ochid: /<chid>` references a change in the **app repo**.
- `ochid: /.claude/<chid>` references a change in the **`.claude`
  repo**.

Trailers are blank-line-separated `key: value` lines at the end of
the commit body:

```
ochid: /.claude/xvzvruqo   # points to a .claude repo change
ochid: /wtpmottv           # points to an app repo change
```

How many, and which direction:

- **Code-side commits** each carry one
  `ochid: /.claude/<.claude-chid>` — the `.claude` change ID.
- **The `.claude` commit** carries one `ochid: /<code-chid>` per
  code commit in that push. More than one occurs on a Merge
  non-ff close-out (one ochid per Work commit in the cycle).

Use `vc-x1 chid -s code,bot -L` to capture the change IDs (first
line app, second `.claude`).

### Resolvability

jj changeIDs are generated at `jj git init` time and are not
stored in the repo, so ochid references resolve only for tools
with access to the local jj repo (e.g. vc-x1).

### .vc-config.toml

Each repo contains a `.vc-config.toml` that identifies its
location within the workspace, so tools resolve these paths
without repeating the workspace path in every trailer:

```toml
# In vc-template-x1 (workspace root):
[workspace]
path = "/"

# In .claude (sub-repo):
[workspace]
path = "/.claude"
```

## Prose form

input.old_string:
### Resolvability

input.new_string:
### Resolvability

A change ID travels with its commit: a **pushed** commit resolves
to the same chid in every clone — cloning the `.claude` repo gave
the published `main` tip the same chid as an existing clone. The
bot thinks jj carries the change ID in the git commit object, so
it survives `jj git clone` / fetch.

The local-only case is the **working-copy `@`**: jj mints a fresh
random chid for `@` in each clone, so an unpushed `@` is never a
stable ochid target. This is why a `.claude` ochid names `@-`
(the last committed change), not `@`.

lastPrompt: What is wrong with cycle-protocol.md?

aiTitle: Create generic documentation files and reduce duplication

content.text:
Audited it — here's what's wrong, most-concrete first:

## 1. Genericity leak: `vc-x1` used as *this project's own* binary
Of the ~15 `vc-x1` mentions, almost all refer to the **companion tool** you run (`vc-x1 push`, `vc-x1 finalize`, `.vc-x1/push-state.toml`) — those are fine. But **two** refer to the *project's own* binary/crate, which a template must not hardcode:

- **Line 72–73:** "so the lockfile's **`vc-x1`** version tracks `Cargo.toml`" — that's *this project's* crate version, not the tool.
- **Line 155:** "Bump `Cargo.toml` … so **`vc-x1 -V`** reports the active phase" — that's *this project's* binary reporting its version.

In the real `vc-x1` repo these coincide (the project *is* vc-x1), so copying the doc into the template silently carries the wrong name. Should be generic (e.g. `<your-crate>` / `<your-binary> -V`).

## 2. Assumes a Rust crate that doesn't exist
The whole Preparation + per-commit flow leans on Cargo: "Bump `Cargo.toml`" (13, 70), "Update `Cargo.lock`" (71–73), and the cargo cycle `fmt/clippy/test/install` (169–174). But this template has **no `Cargo.toml` / `src/`** — none of those steps can run as written. README's new *Assumptions* section now acknowledges this; cycle-protocol.md — the doc that depends on it most — neither flags the assumption nor links to it.

## 3. `# References` section is off-convention and duplicative

- Its **third bullet isn't a reference at all** — "The per-commit `cargo test --bins` gate exists because…" restates the rationale already given verbatim in *Sub-cycle ladders* (the `cargo test --bins` "Non-negotiable" step). Duplicated narrative, and in the wrong section.

---

A lesser note: the commit template's `ochid: /.claude/<chid>` (~line 195) is just a placeholder — it's fine since it defers to AGENTS.md, which now carries the `@-` / 12-char rule we sorted out this session.

content.text:
That reframes my audit — I was reviewing it as if this were a Rust binary project, which it isn't. Going through your three points:

**1. The `vc-x1` references aren't leaks.** You're right — I had the relationship backwards. `vc-x1` is the *tool that produced* AGENTS.md and everything it references; this project neither *is* vc-x1 nor ships a binary. So mentioning `vc-x1` is correct and useful (it suggests the tool to use). The actual defect is narrower than I said: lines 72 and 155 don't just *mention* vc-x1, they imply **this project has a versioned binary** — "the lockfile's `vc-x1` version," "`vc-x1 -V` reports the active phase at build time." That's the thing to fix: keep the suggestion to use vc-x1, drop the implication that the project has its own binary/crate version.

**2. Rust is an example, not the medium.** This is the big one, and it propagates further than cycle-protocol.md. The dual-repo convention — cross-linking the "what" (artifact) to the "why/how" (conversation) — is medium-agnostic: code, a painting, a song, a book, a screenplay. The "per-commit **cargo** cycle" is really "**validate/produce the artifact**," and Rust's fmt/clippy/test/install is just *one* instantiation. So the fix isn't "note the Rust prerequisite" (which is how I'd framed it, and how the **README *Assumptions* section I added this session** frames it — that bullet is now mis-cast and should be revisited). The fix is to present Rust/cargo as a clearly-labeled *example* and state the general shape around it.

**3. The conventions are flexible.** Fair — "off-convention" was too strong. Inline links/anchors and `[N]` references are interchangeable, so the `# References`-as-See-also is fine. The only thing I'd still stand by is *non-stylistic*: the third bullet there duplicates the `cargo test --bins` rationale already stated in *Sub-cycle ladders* — and even that, by your "nothing is hard and fast," is a judgment call, not an error.

**Where I land:** the real issue is a single root cause — the docs inherited a **Rust/binary-centric frame** from vc-x1's own origin, and present medium-specific specifics (Cargo.toml versioning, the cargo cycle, `-V`) as if they were the general protocol. The repair is reframing, not find-replace:

- Make the medium-agnostic nature explicit up front (the artifact is whatever the bot generates).
- Demote the cargo cycle and Cargo.toml versioning to a worked **example** — I'd *keep* a concrete example (abstract-only docs are harder to follow), just labeled as one.
- Rephrase 72 / 155 so they don't presume a project binary.
- Revisit the README *Assumptions* bullet to match (vc-x1 = the tool; Rust = an example medium).

One genuine open question before touching anything: **where does the version live for a non-code medium?** `X.Y.Z-N` is the spine of the whole cycle, but "bump `Cargo.toml`" is Rust's storage location. For a book it might be frontmatter; for a song, an export metadata field. Do you want the protocol to abstract that ("bump the version wherever your project records it; Rust → Cargo.toml"), or keep code as the canonical example and treat other media as variations?

Want me to draft the reframing along these lines, or talk through the version-location question first?

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# vc-template-x1

This is the main repo of a dual-repo convention for using
a bot to help in the development of a coding project. The goal
is that this main repo contains the "what", while the partner
bot repo contains "why" and "how". The key to the convention
is each change is cross-referenced to the other. Thus there
is a coherent story of the development of the project across time.

The beginnings of that tool is [vc-x1](https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1)
which currently does achieve this goal, but is being used as a
first test bed.

## This is a template — customize freely

`CLAUDE.md` and this `README.md` are just starting points.
Read both and edit either to match the conventions you want for your
project. In particular, decisions like the `cargo install` behavior
(default re-resolve vs `--locked`) under [Releasing](#releasing) are
yours to make — pick what fits and update the docs (and the
per-commit cargo cycle) so the choice is recorded for future readers.

## Assumptions

The workflow documented here presumes two things this template
does not itself ship:

- **A Rust crate.** The per-commit cargo cycle (`cargo fmt` /
  `clippy` / `test` / `install`) and the `Cargo.toml` version
  bumps assume you have added a crate; the template carries docs
  and notes only (no `Cargo.toml` / `src/` yet).
- **The `vc-x1` companion tool.** Steps that invoke `vc-x1`
  (`fix-todo`, `validate-todo`, `chid`, `push`, `finalize`) need
  [vc-x1](https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1) installed; the
  underlying jj steps are documented so the flow also works
  without it.

## Cloning

**TBD.** Earlier text here described the bot session repo as a git
submodule; that is stale — it is a standalone repo, cloned with
`jj git clone`, not a submodule. Bootstrapping a new project from a
template is moving to `vc-x1 init <template-url>`, which reads a
`.init-file-list.txt` from the template to lay down the generic
files. This section will be rewritten once that lands.

## Releasing

**`cargo install` lockfile gotcha.** If you install your project's
binary with `cargo install --path .`, also pass `--locked`. By
default `cargo install` ignores `Cargo.lock` and re-resolves
dependencies from scratch, so it can silently build the binary from
a different — possibly broken — dependency graph than `cargo build`
/ `cargo test` ran against.

| command | reads `Cargo.lock`? | re-resolves? |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `cargo build` / `test` / `run`  | yes     | only if `Cargo.toml` changed |
| `cargo build --locked` (etc.)   | yes     | never; errors if it would need to |
| `cargo install` (default)       | **no**  | always, from scratch |
| `cargo install --locked`        | yes     | never; errors if it would need to |

There's no stable cargo config knob to make `--locked` the default
for `cargo install`, so the discipline lives in the per-commit cargo
cycle and shell aliases (e.g. `alias ci='cargo install --locked'`).

The trade-off is predictable-as-tested (`--locked`) vs picking up
upstream fixes via fresh resolves (default). Either is defensible —
pick what fits your project, then edit this section and the
per-commit cargo cycle to match. Background:
[cargo#7169](https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/issues/7169),
[cargo#9436](https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/issues/9436).

## jj Tips for Git Users

This project uses [Jujutsu (jj)](https://docs.jj-vcs.dev/latest/)
alongside git. New to jj? See
[Steve Klabnik](https://github.com/steveklabnik)'s
[Jujutsu tutorial](https://steveklabnik.github.io/jujutsu-tutorial).

Repo-specific how-tos — initial commit, pushing, modifying and
force-pushing a commit, revsets, and a useful-commands reference —
live in [notes/jj-tips.md](notes/jj-tips.md).

## Cross-repo Linking with Git Trailers

Commits in each repo use [git trailers](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-interpret-trailers)
to cross-reference their counterpart in the other repo via an
`ochid` (Other Change ID) trailer — the defining mechanism of the
dual-repo convention. For the full definition — trailer syntax,
the example shape, per-commit mechanics, and `.vc-config.toml` —
see
[Cross-repo linking (ochid trailers)](AGENTS.md#cross-repo-linking-ochid-trailers).

## Contributing

Bot-following workflow, commit conventions, and code style are
canonical in [AGENTS.md](AGENTS.md) (which `CLAUDE.md` imports)
and [notes/cycle-protocol.md](notes/cycle-protocol.md):

- [Cycle numbering](notes/cycle-protocol.md#numbering) — the
  `X.Y.Z-N` phase-suffix convention (Preparation / Work /
  Close-out).
- [Commit description](notes/cycle-protocol.md#commit-description)
  — Conventional Commits + `(version)` suffix and per-repo body
  shape.
- [Per-commit flow](notes/cycle-protocol.md#per-commit-flow) —
  the cargo cycle plus the Work and Commit-Description review
  gates.
- [Code Conventions](AGENTS.md#code-conventions) — doc comments on
  every file / fn / method, `// OK: …` on `unwrap*` calls,
  ask-on-ambiguity, stuck detection.

Task tracking and release details live under [notes/](notes/):
near-term tasks in [notes/todo.md](notes/todo.md), per-release
details in `notes/chores/chores-*.md`, and notes-specific
formatting rules in [notes/README.md](notes/README.md).

## License

- Apache License, Version 2.0 ([LICENSE-APACHE](LICENSE-APACHE) or http://apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0)
- MIT license ([LICENSE-MIT](LICENSE-MIT) or http://opensource.org/licenses/MIT)

### Contribution

Unless you explicitly state otherwise, any contribution intentionally submitted
for inclusion in the work by you, as defined in the Apache-2.0 license, shall
be dual licensed as above, without any additional terms or conditions.

| `cargo build` / `test` / `run`  | yes     | only if `Cargo.toml` changed |
| `cargo build --locked` (etc.)   | yes     | never; errors if it would need to |
| `cargo install` (default)       | **no**  | always, from scratch |
| `cargo install --locked`        | yes     | never; errors if it would need to |

There's no stable cargo config knob to make `--locked` the default
for `cargo install`, so the discipline lives in the per-commit cargo
cycle and shell aliases (e.g. `alias ci='cargo install --locked'`).

The trade-off is predictable-as-tested (`--locked`) vs picking up
upstream fixes via fresh resolves (default). Either is defensible —
pick what fits your project, then edit this section and the
per-commit cargo cycle to match. Background:
[cargo#7169](https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/issues/7169),
[cargo#9436](https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/issues/9436).

## jj Tips for Git Users

This project uses [Jujutsu (jj)](https://docs.jj-vcs.dev/latest/)
alongside git. New to jj? See
[Steve Klabnik](https://github.com/steveklabnik)'s
[Jujutsu tutorial](https://steveklabnik.github.io/jujutsu-tutorial).

Repo-specific how-tos — initial commit, pushing, modifying and
force-pushing a commit, revsets, and a useful-commands reference —
live in [notes/jj-tips.md](notes/jj-tips.md).

## Cross-repo Linking with Git Trailers

Commits in each repo use [git trailers](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-interpret-trailers)
to cross-reference their counterpart in the other repo via an
`ochid` (Other Change ID) trailer — the defining mechanism of the
dual-repo convention. For the full definition — trailer syntax,
the example shape, per-commit mechanics, and `.vc-config.toml` —
see
[Cross-repo linking (ochid trailers)](AGENTS.md#cross-repo-linking-ochid-trailers).

## Contributing

Bot-following workflow, commit conventions, and code style are
canonical in [AGENTS.md](AGENTS.md) (which `CLAUDE.md` imports)
and [notes/cycle-protocol.md](notes/cycle-protocol.md):

- [Cycle numbering](notes/cycle-protocol.md#numbering) — the
  `X.Y.Z-N` phase-suffix convention (Preparation / Work /
  Close-out).
- [Commit description](notes/cycle-protocol.md#commit-description)
  — Conventional Commits + `(version)` suffix and per-repo body
  shape.
- [Per-commit flow](notes/cycle-protocol.md#per-commit-flow) —
  the cargo cycle plus the Work and Commit-Description review
  gates.
- [Code Conventions](AGENTS.md#code-conventions) — doc comments on
  every file / fn / method, `// OK: …` on `unwrap*` calls,
  ask-on-ambiguity, stuck detection.

Task tracking and release details live under [notes/](notes/):
near-term tasks in [notes/todo.md](notes/todo.md), per-release
details in `notes/chores/chores-*.md`, and notes-specific
formatting rules in [notes/README.md](notes/README.md).

aiTitle: Create generic documentation files and reduce duplication

message.content: The longer form is fine for now, one Q. These are instructions primarily for you with but shared with the user. If I took this as it is right now for a Rust project would it work or would it be to general?

content.text:
Honest answer: **yes, it would work for a Rust project — it didn't go too general.** The reframing wrapped the Rust specifics in medium-agnostic language but kept every concrete Rust command as the running example, so a bot following it has real steps to execute, not just abstractions:

- Per-commit step 4 still spells out `cargo fmt` → `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings` → `cargo test` → `cargo install --path . --locked`.
- Version bump says "`Cargo.toml` for a Rust project."
- The `Cargo.lock` step, the `cargo test --bins` ladder gate, and the README *Releasing* `--locked` gotcha are all intact and Rust-concrete.

So nothing operational was hollowed out. A bot wouldn't be left guessing what to run.

The friction you'd hit isn't "too general" — it's that **the template is itself non-Rust**, so taking it "as is" leaves two small chores the adopter must do (which your "each adopter edits AGENTS.md to fit their project" model already expects):

1. **No crate ships.** There's no `Cargo.toml`/`src/` — you'd `cargo init` and add the real crate yourself before the cargo cycle has anything to act on.
2. **`version.toml` is the wrong home for Rust.** This repo ships `version.toml`; a Rust project versions in `Cargo.toml`. So you'd retire `version.toml` and trim the "this repo: `version.toml`" asides to point at `Cargo.toml`. Until you do, the docs name *two* version locations — workable, but mildly confusing if read literally.

Net: a bot could run a Rust cycle from these instructions today, after you add the crate. The only "too general" smell is the dual version-location mention (#2), and that resolves the moment an adopter does the one-line customization the template is designed to invite.

If you want it to read cleaner for the *Rust* reader specifically, I could flip the framing so Rust/`Cargo.toml` is the primary and `version.toml` is the parenthetical "(this template has no crate, so it uses…)" — but that partly undoes the medium-agnostic intent. Your call; just answering the question for now.

message.content: No it's fine for now, it's going to continue to evolve :)

file.content:
# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

When a `## Todo` item is picked up, its text moves here: the
problem overview and its list of things to do. That is followed
by the "plan" — a bulleted list of the development "ladder":
   - 0.xx.y-0 blah (done)
   - 0.xx.y-1 blah blah (current)
   - 0.xx.y-2 blah blah blah
   - 0.xx.y close-out and validation

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).


- chore: dummy chore (TBD) [[2]]

## Done

Completed tasks are moved from `## Todo` to here, `## Done`, as they are completed
and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.

- docs: completed dummy chore (0.1.0) [[1]]

# References

[1]: chores/chores-01.md#docs-completed-dummy-chore-010
[2]: chores/chores-01.md#chore-dummy-chore-tbd

content.text:
I've got the SHAs and remote (`github.com/winksaville/vc-template-x1`), so the done.md entries with commit references are ready to write. One judgment call before I do, because of the dummy template content:

This session shipped two commits:
- `docs: restructure template docs and notes` — `bd2902617c2f`
- `docs: reframe convention as medium-agnostic` — `ad105f0f2ddc`

And `todo.md` currently holds **dummy template examples** (`## Done`: "docs: completed dummy chore", `## Todo`: "chore: dummy chore (TBD)") that demonstrate the format for adopters. So "retire this session's work" forks two ways:

- **Option A (recommended):** Record both real commits in **done.md** (archived, with `[[N]]` commit references → URL + 40-hex SHA). Leave the dummy entries in `todo.md` untouched as the template's format examples. → done.md becomes the real history; todo.md stays a clean illustrative template.
- **Option B:** Treat it like a normal close-out — add the two real commits to `todo.md > ## Done`, and retire the older **dummy** "completed" entry down to done.md. → todo.md's `## Done` shows the real recent work; the dummy moves to the archive.

I lean A: this repo *is* the template, so the dummies are worth keeping as live examples, and shipped work belongs in the archive. Which way do you want it?

message.content: No, since this is the template no changes but do commit bot repo, it'll be "the" record for the commits, your thoughts?
No, since this is the template no changes to todo or done. But do commit bot repo, it'll be "the" record for the commits, your thoughts?

content.text:
My thoughts — I think it's the right call, with two caveats to decide on.

**Why it works:** the `.claude` repo *is* the "why/how" — the conversation that produced both commits. One `.claude` commit covering this whole session, carrying a back-`ochid` to each app commit, is exactly the `.claude`-side shape AGENTS.md describes ("the `.claude` commit carries one `ochid: /<code-chid>` per code commit"). So that commit becomes the authoritative record linking the session to `bd2902617c2f` and `ad105f0f2ddc`. Cleaner than polluting the template's todo/done, which you're right to keep as illustrative.

**Caveat 1 — the link is one-directional.** Both app commits are already pushed with `ochid: /.claude/ortvnxqmmpuw` — i.e. pointing at the *previous* `.claude` tip, not the new commit we're about to make. So the new `.claude` commit will point forward at them correctly, but they won't point back at it (they point at the prior one). Making it fully bidirectional means force-pushing two immutable, already-published app commits to rewrite a trailer — a remote rewrite. My vote: **accept the asymmetry** — the `.claude` commit is "the record," and rewriting published history for one trailer isn't worth it. (This is just the chicken-and-egg surfacing exactly where you said it would.)

**Caveat 2 — the oversized session log.** At session start, `jj` refused to snapshot `.claude/e5ec0e9d-….jsonl` (1.05 MiB > jj's 1.0 MiB default). If we commit `.claude` as-is, **that file is silently excluded** — so "the record" would be missing a chunk of the session. Before committing we should decide:
- **Raise the limit** for the `.claude` repo (`jj config set --repo snapshot.max-new-file-size …`) so the full log is captured — my lean, since the point is a complete record; or
- **gitignore it** and accept a partial record.

If you're good with "accept asymmetry + raise the snapshot limit," I'll: grab the two app chids (12-char), bump the `.claude` snapshot limit, then commit `.claude` with both `ochid: /<app-chid>` trailers. Sound right?

lastPrompt: No, since this is the template no changes to todo or done. But do commit bot repo, it'll be "the" record for the commits, your thoughts?

Caveat 2: I raised the limit immeidately after you pointed that out, so I assume there is no lost data, if there is so be it.

content.text:
Done — stopping here as you asked. 🙂

**Current state:**
- **App repo** — `main@origin` at `ad105f0f` (both session commits pushed, done).
- **`.claude` repo** — new session-record commit **`ppmwxuno` / `7cab9f2f`** (`docs: template review + medium-agnostic reframe`) created **locally**, carrying `ochid: /wpzrkyuzvwnt` and `ochid: /uqwnzplntzko`. `.claude`'s `main@origin` is still at `ortvnxqm` — **not pushed**. Fresh empty `@` on top.

**Next steps to discuss:**

1. **Push `.claude`?** This is the main one. Right now the session record is local-only — so it isn't durable, shareable, or clonable, and its chid (`ppmwxuno`) won't resolve for anyone else until pushed. ===== finalize --help =====
vc-x1 0.65.0

Squash, set bookmark, and/or push a jj repo.

Designed for the bot to atomically finalize its session repo:
--detach exits immediately, --delay waits for trailing writes,
--squash folds them in, --bookmark + --push sends it upstream.
Every flag is opt-in. See README.md for details.

Usage: vc-x1 finalize [OPTIONS]

Options:
      --repo <REPO>
          Path to jj repo
          
          [default: .]

  -V, --version
          Print the `vc-x1 X.Y.Z` banner as the first line, then continue. With
          no subcommand, prints the banner and exits.
          
          Replaces clap's auto-version (which would exit after printing): the
          banner now rides along with normal subcommand execution rather than
          gating it, so scripts can capture the version *and* the command's
          output in one invocation.

      --squash [<SOURCE,TARGET>]
          Squash SOURCE into TARGET [default: @,@-]

  -v, --verbose...
          Verbose output: -v debug, -vv trace

      --delay <DELAY>
          Seconds to wait before squashing
          
          [default: 10]

      --log <LOG>
          Log file path (captures all levels)

      --push <BOOKMARK>
          Existing bookmark to push to remote

      --detach
          Detach and run in the background

  -h, --help
          Print help (see a summary with '-h')

content.text:
Confirmed and I'm on board. `vc-x1 0.65.0` is installed, and `--help` settles my one worry: `--squash` defaults to `@,@-`, so it folds the trailing-data working copy (`@` = `vsyormnu`, no description) **into** `ppmwxuno` (`@-`). Since the source has no description, the squash **keeps `ppmwxuno`'s message and both `ochid` trailers** — no clobber. So this does exactly what we want: capture the session tail, then publish the record as one commit.

So my thoughts: yes, finalize beats a plain push here — it closes the snapshot-completeness gap I flagged, and the squash is non-destructive to the trailer work.

Exact command I'd run:
```
vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --squash --push main --delay 10 --detach --log /tmp/vc-x1-finalize.log
```
- `--delay 10` waits for the harness to flush trailing writes into `@`; `--squash` folds them into `ppmwxuno`; `--push main` fast-forwards `.claude`'s `main` (`ortvnxqm → ppmwxuno`) to origin; `--detach` returns immediately; `--log` lets us inspect the result afterward.

Two process notes, since this is the canonical detached finalize:
- It's a hard stop — I'll put **all** closing words *before* the invoke and stay completely silent after it returns (the `--delay` snapshot is meant to capture this turn's tail, including my pre-invoke words; a post-invoke summary is the known anti-pattern).
- Per the recovery guidance, if `@` is still non-empty afterward (data landing after the snapshot), a second finalize pass mops it up — you can just tell me to re-run.

Shall I run it?

Both repos will then have their counterpart record. After the tool returns I'll stay silent per the finalize rule; if `@` is non-empty when you next look (trailing data landing post-snapshot), just say "re-run" and I'll do a second finalize pass. The throwaway clone at `/tmp/vc-template-x1.claude` is still there whenever you want it gone.
